attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of pain, numbness, and tingling in his right arm and hand. The symptoms worsen when he raises his arm above shoulder level. He developed headaches four-months ago that are relieved with paracetamol, and he does not have a previous history of headaches. He works as an electrician and often performs overhead tasks. There is no history of trauma.

He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is decreased sensation along the medial aspect of his right forearm and hand. There was no muscle wasting and no skin changes. A chest X-ray is performed (Image), which shows a cervical rib on the right side.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediate surgical resection of the cervical rib
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms suggestive of thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS), likely neurogenic TOS given the sensory symptoms in the arm and hand. The brachial plexus or subclavian vessels are compressed as it exits, and symptoms will vary on the degree of compression for neurogenic, venous or arterial TOS. The chest X-ray revealed a cervical rib which only occurs in 0.5% of the population however it is a known anatomical cause of TOS. The headache is likely secondary to spasm of the paraspinal and trapezius muscles in response to upper brachial plexus involvement.

Physical therapy and lifestyle modifications are the most appropriate first-line treatment for thoracic outlet syndrome. This conservative approach aims to strengthen the muscles supporting the shoulder girdle, improve posture, and teach the patient how to avoid positions that exacerbate symptoms. For this electrician, it would include guidance on modifying work techniques to reduce overhead activities. Whilst difficult due to the physical nature of an electrician's work, it is worthwhile to attempt conservative measure before more invasive interventions.

Immediate surgical resection of the cervical rib is not the first-line treatment for TOS. Surgery is typically reserved for cases that do not respond to conservative management or have severe, progressive neurological deficits. It carries risks and should not be undertaken without first attempting non-invasive treatments.

Referral for nerve conduction studies can be helpful in some cases of TOS, particularly to rule out other conditions like carpal tunnel syndrome. However, it is not the most appropriate next step given the clear clinical picture and radiographic evidence of a cervical rib.

Prescription of gabapentin might be considered for symptom management in some cases of TOS, but it is not the most appropriate first-line treatment. The focus should be on addressing the underlying cause through conservative measures before resorting to medication.

Cervical spine MRI is not necessary at this stage. The chest X-ray has already revealed the likely cause of the patient's symptoms (the cervical rib), and there are no signs or symptoms suggesting a primary cervical spine pathology.

If conservative management fails to improve symptoms after six to twelve weeks of physiotherapy, further investigations (MRI and nerve conduction studies) or surgical consultation may be considered. However, many patients with TOS experience significant improvement with physical therapy and lifestyle modifications alone.
learningPoints: Thoracic outlet syndrome can be caused by a cervical rib, presenting with neurogenic symptoms in the upper limb.
Conservative management, including physical therapy and lifestyle modifications, is the first-line treatment for thoracic outlet syndrome.
references: Ho, K. Guazzo L. Muller J. Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome: When to consider the diagnosis and current management options. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2023: 52 (9). Available in: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2023/september/neurogenic-thoracic-outlet-syndrome
Hacking C. Thoracic Outlet Syndrome. Radiopaedia. 2024. Available in: https://radiopaedia.org/articles/thoracic-outlet-syndrome?lang=us
